rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the statement itself per climate_screen=explicit_self. The text focuses on sewer/stormwater system upgrades for water quality protection: 'reduce pollution load discharged to rivers or the sea' and 'capture initial stormwater mixed with sanitary sewage' plus 'introduction of a separate sewer system'. These are water infrastructure and overflow/stormwater management actions, aligning better with Adaptation/resilience than with Resource Efficiency outcomes (hence the gate failure for missing Resource Efficiency outcome tokens).
policy_statement: TMG will reduce pollution loads by continuously improving combined sewer systems (including capturing initial stormwater and introducing separate sewer systems for some facilities/development districts).
mechanism_description: The plan strives to improve combined sewer systems by capturing initial stormwater mixed with sewage and introducing separate sewers in some areas, reducing pollution loads discharged to rivers or the sea.
primary_causal_pathway: Urban water infrastructure upgrade → ↓ combined sewer overflows/untreated discharges → ↓ water pollution load (improved water quality)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Cities retrofit/upgrade combined sewer networks (e.g., first-flush capture, storage/treatment capacity, partial sewer separation) to prevent stormwater+sewage from discharging untreated during storms. (2) Mediating physical process: Reduced hydraulic overload and bypass events lowers the frequency/volume of combined sewer overflows; captured flows are conveyed to treatment rather than released directly. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Cleaner receiving waters and more robust urban water services under heavy rainfall conditions; any GHG effects are generally indirect and secondary.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is improved stormwater/wastewater management that reduces combined sewer overflows and associated contamination during heavy rainfall events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Infrastructure upgrades reduce combined sewer overflow discharges, leading to lower pollutant loads and improved water quality (service performance/efficiency of the urban water system).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The described mechanism is not primarily a direct GHG mitigation pathway (no principal step of reducing fuel use or methane capture) and not explicitly an adaptation pathway framed around reducing exposure/impacts (even if it can help during heavy rain). Its core outcome is reduced pollution per unit of storm event/service provided—best captured as Resource Efficiency (improved water system performance with reduced environmental loading).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy explicitly frames sewer upgrades as flood-risk reduction/heat-health protection under climate change, it could be Adaptation at the policy level; if it includes energy optimization or biogas capture at treatment, it could add Mitigation as a secondary effect. Those are not inherent to this canonical mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that improves combined sewer systems to capture first flush and/or separate sewers shares the primary effect of reducing untreated discharges and pollution loads; that remains the dominant pathway across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., Adaptation co-benefit via reduced contamination after extreme rainfall) must be determined from policy-specific evidence, not assumed from the mechanism alone.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be classified as Adaptation when the dominant stated goal is climate-resilient drainage/flood and contamination risk reduction under extreme rainfall; could have Mitigation co-benefits if upgrades materially change wastewater treatment energy use or methane emissions, but that is not implied by “pollution load reduction” alone.
key_indicators: "continuous improvement of the combined sewer system"; "development of facilities to capture initial stormwater"; "introduction of a separate sewer system"; "reduce pollution load discharged to rivers or the sea"
co_benefits: Improved public health and amenity from cleaner waterways; reduced flood/CSO impacts during heavy rainfall events
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be framed as a planning/strategy commitment (“Strive to…”) rather than a funded capital program, but the specific actions described (facility development, sewer separation) most strongly indicate an infrastructure investment measure.
